title: Boarding a loft without compressing insulation
subtitle: Raising loft boards on stilts to protect insulation value
excerpt: Boards laid directly on top of mineral wool compress it. Compressed insulation performs far worse than uncompressed: 270 mm squeezed to 50 mm loses most of its resistance. Proprietary loft boarding supports sit over the joists and carry board weight without touching the wool.
sourceNotes: BRE Good Repair Guide on loft storage and insulation compression. Energy Saving Trust guidance on maintaining loft insulation depth under storage boards. LABC technical guidance on loft boarding systems.

content.text: Standard loft boarding sits on the tops of the joists, which are typically 100 mm deep. A second layer of
rolled at 90 degrees to the joists rises above the joist tops by 170 mm to reach 270 mm total. Boards placed over that second layer compress it down to 50 or 60 mm. At that depth, the

increases and the thermal resistance drops to roughly the level of the old unimproved insulation. Loft boarding supports raise the boards 170 to 250 mm above the joists, leaving the wool uncompressed underneath.

attrs.body: A rough rule: compressing mineral wool to half its free thickness reduces its R-value by approximately 30-40%, not 50%. The wool densifies and its lambda increases. Compressing 270 mm to 60 mm (a factor of 4.5) reduces R-value by approximately 70%. The remaining 60 mm of compressed wool is not much better than the original 100 mm pre-retrofit.
attrs.title: How much compression matters

content.text: Proprietary loft boarding supports (sold as LoftLegs, Loft Leg, or similar) are plastic stilts that slot over or fix to joist tops and rise 170 mm or 250 mm above them. The boards sit on the stilt heads, transferring load through the stilts to the joists without bearing on the insulation.

Supports are available in 170 mm height (for a 100 mm joist with 170 mm top-up insulation, giving 270 mm total) and 250 mm (for a 100 mm joist with 250 mm top-up, giving 350 mm total). Check the support height matches the insulation depth you are installing.
Install the mineral wool to the full 270 mm depth before fitting any supports or boards. All eaves ventilator trays must already be in place and cables pulled up over the insulation.
Step 2, fit the loft boarding supports.

Press supports onto joist tops at the board span spacing required by the board manufacturer (typically 400 or 600 mm). Keep your weight on the joists throughout. Use a kneeling board to distribute your weight and avoid sinking into the insulation between joists.
Step 3, lay boards on the supports.

18 mm chipboard or OSB sheets typically come in 1200 x 600 mm or 2400 x 600 mm panels. Cut with a handsaw or circular saw to fit the boarded area. Leave a 5-10 mm gap between board edges to allow for seasonal movement. Secure boards to supports with screws through pre-drilled holes if the support design requires it.
Step 4, check access to the hatch.

The board level is now higher than the joist tops. The loft hatch drop and any fixed ladder or steps must accommodate the new floor height. Proprietary insulated hatch boxes are designed for this configuration.

Loft boarding supports do not increase the structural load capacity of the joists, which are sized for ceiling loads only. Typical loft joists (50 x 100 mm at 400 mm centres) carry approximately 25 kg/m² safely for lightweight storage. Heavy items (stacked books, archived files, dense equipment) will exceed this. If significant loads are planned, a structural engineer should assess joist capacity before boarding.

items.fix: Check supports are seated on joists (not between them). Reduce support spacing or increase board thickness to 22 mm.
items.cause: Supports spaced too far apart for the board thickness, or supports not located on joist tops

items.fix: Refluff the compressed wool. Ensure cables and pipe lagging are positioned before the second insulation layer goes down.
items.cause: Weight placed directly on insulation between joists during installation, or cables pinned the wool down
